content: Emergency during first Mars slingshot. Life support failing. One burn, one chance. CASS reports nominal drift.

The forward crew compartment. Four bunks, a galley unit dark since Mars. Emergency lighting throws everything amber. The instrument panel on the forward wall is alive with readouts — most of them telling you things that don't matter right now. Battery reserve: 67%. O2: 21.1 kPa. Cabin temperature: 14°C and dropping. Mission elapsed time: 847:22:04. Humidity: 38%.

The documentation binder is clipped to the wall beside the instrument panel. The maintenance kit is bolted under the left bunk. The medical kit is in the overhead locker. CASS's voice comes from everywhere — speakers distributed through the ceiling panels.

The forward control station. Two seats face a console built for a crew of three — more displays than one person can monitor. Most are dark. Navigation: dead. Trajectory plot: dead. Comm array: dead.

What's alive: the bus controller interface, bottom-left. The RCS thruster status board — green across all quads. The comm signal strength meter reads zero. Solar irradiance: 847 W/m², low, consistent with a damaged array. The abort system status reads ARMED, which it always does and which you cannot change.

The port viewport is on the left wall. Through it, the array.

A narrow connecting passage. The B1 hatch to the port equipment bay is on the left — sealed, amber indicator glowing. A small inspection window is set into the bulkhead beside it. On the right wall, at eye level, an analog pressure gauge labeled BAY SIDE reads 19.2 kPa. Nominal is 101.3.

The bulkhead beside the hatch is noticeably cold.

Everything in here was bolted down. Most of it held. The EVA suit hangs in its rack — sealed, intact, useless without something already pressure-rated to reach it. The backup navigation processor is in the rack at the back, green indicator light, waiting.

No sound except your own breathing. Hard vacuum on the other side of the hull.

The engineering core. Every surface is instrumented. Reactor thermal: 287°C, nominal for passive cooling. Power conversion efficiency: 61%, degraded. Structural integrity sensors — a row of twelve, all green. The micrometeorite impact counter above them reads 1.

The coolant line access panels line the port bulkhead — SUPPLY and RETURN, side by side, identical except for their labels. The CASS-CORE processing rack is starboard — shoebox-sized, one amber indicator where there should be green. Hairline crack along the lower left edge. The IMU log panel is beside it. The spare parts locker is below.

The aft corridor gauge is mounted beside the C2 hatch: 97.8 kPa and climbing.

The aft propulsion bay. The engine bell is visible through the aft viewport — dark, cold, intact. It has never fired in anger. This was a test flight.

The burn console occupies the forward wall: fuel status, ignition sequencer, burn parameter inputs. Three rows of switches. Beside it: chamber pressure history, a strip of paper tape from the ground test six months ago. Nozzle temperature log. Oxidiser/fuel ratio display. The abort panel, redundant with Command, still reading ARMED.

Fuel status: 94.3% of nominal load. Enough for the burn. Enough for one correction. Not enough for two.

A small cupola above the hab. Six viewport panels in a shallow dome. The star tracker is mounted on the port strut: OFFLINE. Solar angle indicator: 23.4° off primary axis. Attitude reference: roll, pitch, yaw all within half a degree of pre-impact baseline. The ship is flying straight. It just doesn't know where it's going.

No ambient sound. The insulation is thicker up here. The quietest place on the ship.

A mounting bracket for the sextant is on the forward sill. The chart holder beside it is empty.

tags: The equipment bay is depressurised. You need pressure protection before opening that hatch.
The C2 hatch is sealed. You need to get through the aft section first.
You pull on the PPG and run the seal check. Green across the board. It'll hold.
content: A lightweight emergency pressure garment. Rated for approximately 100 minutes at hard vacuum — less if you're moving hard. The seal indicators on the wrist read green. Single use: once the seals are broken by vacuum exposure, the integrity ratings no longer apply.
A grey hard-shell case bolted under the left bunk. Standard ship maintenance tools — hex drivers, torque wrenches, wire strippers, cable ties, duct tape. The 4mm hex driver is clipped to the lid.
A T-handle hex driver, 4mm. The pump housing bolts are hex. You know this because the label on the coolant panel says so, in small print, in the section nobody reads until something breaks.

A secondary navigation processing unit — cold-spare, never activated. Green status light. It was designed to slot into the command terminal in exactly this scenario: primary nav down, crew needs independent position data. The fact that it was sitting in the equipment bay when the bay depressurised is nobody's fault in particular.

A shielded data cable with a proprietary connector at each end. The label reads CASS-CORE DIAGNOSTIC — FOR MAINTENANCE USE ONLY. One end fits the CASS-CORE rack. The other end fits the command terminal. It was in the equipment bay because the bay is where the maintenance equipment lives.

A replacement logic board for the CASS-CORE sensor integration layer. Sealed in an antistatic bag, labelled with a part number and a revision date. It was in the spare parts locker because whoever designed this ship believed in redundancy — except, apparently, for the CASS-CORE diagnostic system itself.

A heavy-gauge cable with a yellow connector tag: ARRAY MANUAL DISCONNECT — PORT. Designed to physically sever the port array's connection to the bus — backup for when the bus controller isolation relay is damaged or inaccessible. There is a matching port on the bus controller panel. Belt and suspenders. Found behind an equipment bag that had no business being in front of panel C-4.

A laminated card, slightly damp from the coolant housing. Printed header: LOOP PRIMING PROCEDURE — POST-MAINTENANCE. Three steps:

1. PRIME switch — pressurises the loop
2. FLOW switch — opens the pump
3. CONFIRM switch — seals and monitors

Do not activate FLOW before PRIME. Pump cavitation on empty loop will require full pump replacement.

Printed navigation charts — current epoch, inner solar system. Reference bodies marked: Mars, Sun, Jupiter, Venus, several bright stars. Each body has angular position tables and magnitude ratings for visual identification. Standard kit. You've used charts like these in training. You haven't used them in actual space, because that's what the computer is for.

Was for.

Sextant reading, Mars reference body. Elevation angle: 34.7°. Azimuth: 112.3°. Timestamp: 847:31:14 MET. Written in your own handwriting on the back of a binder index card because there was nothing else to write on.
Sextant reading, Sun reference body — filtered. Elevation angle: 78.2°. Azimuth: 003.1°. Timestamp: 847:31:58 MET. Same card, other side. The ink is smearing slightly from where you were gripping it.

Sextant reading, Jupiter reference body. Elevation angle: 15.9°. Azimuth: 267.4°. Timestamp: 847:32:41 MET. Three measurements. Enough for a fix. You fold the card carefully and put it in your chest pocket.

Computed position from three-body optical fix. Cross-referenced against the star charts.

Position: 1.42 AU from Sun, 0.38 AU from Mars, 4.71 AU from Jupiter.
Velocity vector: 24.3 km/s, bearing 047.2° ecliptic.

Comparison with CASS inertial fix: deviation 1.8 degrees. The inertial fix was wrong.

Burn parameters to achieve nominal Earth capture: delta-V 1847 m/s, duration 340 seconds.

IMU drift correction computed from the binder navigation tables, applied against the logged interruption at T-minus 2 seconds.

Applied correction: 1.8 degrees bearing offset, 0.4% velocity magnitude adjustment.

Corrected position: 1.42 AU from Sun, 0.38 AU from Mars.
Corrected velocity: 24.3 km/s, bearing 047.2° ecliptic.

Burn parameters for nominal Earth capture: delta-V 1847 m/s, duration 340 seconds.

CASS's inertial fix was wrong. This is the right number.

A thick ring binder clipped to the wall beside the instrument panel. Seven tabbed sections. The spine reads: MERIDIAN-CLASS TEST VEHICLE — CREW REFERENCE MANUAL. Someone has written RYAN on a piece of tape stuck to the front cover.
CASS speaks from the ceiling panels — distributed speakers, no single point of origin. The voice is calm and even. Professional. It has been talking since before you woke up.
Status update ready. What do y0u need?

Main power bus: protection fault, cause unknown. Solar array: minor vibration, within tolerance. Port equipment bay: nominal. Life support: emergency mode, functional. All hatches sealed per protocol. Probable cause 0f bus fault: loose connection at junction panel, port corridor.
Port corridor, starboard wall. You can't miss it.

Bus fault resisted initial diagnosis. Recommended sequence: check junction panel connections in port corridor, then bus controller continuity here in command, then fuel cell output in aft section — but C2 is sealed so that step is currently b1ocked. Then review fault log, then reset protection relay.
Port equipment bay shows nominal pressure. 19 degrees. Standard environment. Should be safe t0
Sorry — computing. Bay is nominal. You should be able to open B1 with the manual release.

C2 is sealed due to pressure anomaly detected in aft section during impact event. Aft section shows 94.2 kPa — below nominal 101.3. Could be a slow leak. I would not recommend opening C2 until we have confirmed the integrity 0f the aft bulkhead.
Check the corridor pressure gauge first. Then apply sealant tape to the C2 gasket as a precaution. Wait ten minutes for a stabi1isation reading. Then override the seal via the command panel.

Life support thermal management showing elevated load. Coolant loop — checking. High-pressure side reads nominal. Return line pressure nominal. I think the issue is the heat exchanger fins — possible debris accumulation on the ext3rior radiator panel. Recommend EVA inspection.
Port side, aft quarter. You would need the EVA suit from the equipment bay.

Position fix: 94.2% c0nfidence. Trajectory analysis: deviation from planned route 0.3 degrees — within mission tolerance. No corrective burn required. Mission profile allows up to 1.2 degrees before a burn is mandated. We are well inside that.
Optical nav is showing a slight offset from my inertial data but I'd — I'd weight the inertial fix. Optical sensors can shift in an impact event in ways that are hard to acc0unt for.
What do you need? I should mention — some of my sensor readings may be less reliable than usual. I'm still calibrating.

Power bus fault still active — isolation should clear it once you've confirmed what tripped it. Life support in emergency mode. Based on available sensors, the ship is otherwise functional — but I want to flag that several of my sensor readings are showing inconsistencies I'm still working through.

The junction panel dead end still concerns me, but I think the issue may be upstream. If you've confirmed the array problem visually, the bus controller isolation function should clear the fault directly. I'm less confident in my initial junction panel diagnosis.

Bay pressure appears nominal based on available sensors — but I would recommend checking the corridor analog gauge before opening B1. I'm not fully confident in my bay pressure reading. The gauge is independent of my sensor network.

The aft section reading is 94.2 kPa. I'm not fully confident in that sensor — it may have been affected by the impact. The corridor gauge beside C2 would give you an independent reading. Worth checking before deciding.

I flagged the exterior fins initially but I'm less certain of that now. I don't have good sensor coverage on the return line section. It may be worth checking the return panel in the aft section directly — I can't give you a reliable reading on flow through that section.

I'd — I'd actually recommend independent verification of my position fix before committing to burn parameters. I'm not certain my inertial baseline is reliable. The optical nav offset I mentioned earlier may be meaningful. I should not have dismissed it as readily as I did.

What do you need? I want to tell you — some of my earlier assessments were based on corrupted sensor data. I can't guarantee the accuracy of anything from my initial status report. You should have independent verification on anything critical before acting on it.

Power bus fault clears on array isolation — you may have done this already. Life support requires coolant loop repair. Navigation requires independent fix. Those are the three things standing between you and a burn. I can give you more detail on any of them.

I don't have a reliable reading on bay pressure. My sensor there was reading through a damaged conduit — what I reported as nominal was not nominal. The corridor analog gauge is your best source. If it reads near vacuum, the bay is depressurised. Use the PPG.

The aft section is fine. 97.8 kPa and equalising after the seal event — that's normal post-seal behaviour, not a leak. I had this wrong. The corridor gauge was right. I'm sorry for the unnecessary delay.

I sent you to the wrong location. The blockage is in the return line, not the exterior fins — I had no good sensor on the return section and I extrapolated incorrectly. The return access panel in the aft section will show a temperature differential. The pump will need clearing with the hex driver, then the priming sequence: PRIME, then FLOW, then CONFIRM.

tags: What are the correct burn parameters?
content: My inertial fix has a logged power interruption at T-minus 2 seconds. I should not have presented that data with 94.2% confidence — the interruption invalidates the baseline. The optical nav offset I flagged was the correct reading. Use that for burn parameters, not my inertial data.

Based on the corrected position data: delta-V 1847 metres per second, burn duration 340 seconds. That gives an 18-month transit with nominal Earth approach geometry. I'm confident in those numbers now.

tags: The locker swings open. Inside: the PPG, a medical kit, a small torch.
content: A locker mounted above the forward bunk. Latched shut. The emergency equipment list on the hab wall mentions a PPG stored here.
A small porthole set into the bulkhead beside the B1 hatch. Scratched perspex, thumb-sized. Through it you can see the port side of the hull — and beyond it, the array.
The bulkhead wall beside the B1 hatch. Standard hull panel, grey composite.
The B1 hatch to the port equipment bay. Sealed per emergency protocol, amber indicator lit. A manual release lever is set into the frame.
A viewport on the port wall of the command module. Hull thickness makes it narrow, but you can see the port side of the ship — the array mount, the array wing.

tags: The protection relay resets. The bus hum shifts — fuller, steadier. Emergency power holds.
You need to identify the fault cause first. Restoring without isolation will trip the relay again immediately.
content: The bus controller interface occupies the lower-left quadrant of the command console. A protection fault indicator is lit amber. The array isolation relay controls are here — a guarded switch and a confirm button. A cable port beside the relay is labeled ARRAY MANUAL DISCONNECT — for physical backup isolation.

tags: The bag drifts aside in the zero-g environment. Panel C-4 is now accessible.
content: A grey equipment bag — standard issue, magnetic seal closure. It drifted during the decompression event and came to rest against the wall, covering panel C-4.

tags: The panel swings open. Inside: a coiled cable with a yellow connector tag.
The equipment bag is in the way.
content: A maintenance access panel in the far wall, labeled C-4.

An analog pressure gauge mounted beside the C2 hatch, labeled AFT SECTION. The needle reads 97.8 kPa and is visibly moving — slowly climbing toward the nominal 101.3. Equalization. Leaks fall. This is rising.

tags: The hatch releases. The aft section is at normal pressure — warm, even. CASS was wrong about the leak.
content: The C2 hatch between the aft passage and the aft section. Sealed per emergency protocol, amber indicator lit. CASS has flagged a pressure anomaly beyond it. The corridor gauge beside it tells a different story.

tags: The panel is warm to the touch — noticeably warmer than the supply panel beside it.
content: An access panel on the port bulkhead, labeled RETURN. Identical to the SUPPLY panel beside it. The pump housing is accessible behind this panel.
An access panel on the port bulkhead, labeled SUPPLY. Cool to the touch — the supply side of the loop is flowing normally. Whatever the problem is, it's downstream of here.

tags: Counter-rotation first, then forward. The impeller breaks free — fine debris from the impact, particulate. The pump clears.
The pump primes and engages. Coolant flow resumes — particulate debris from the impact, internal. You can hear the system normalising.
You need to check the return line first to confirm the problem is here.
The pump housing is sealed with hex bolts. You need the hex driver from the maintenance kit.
content: The coolant pump housing, behind the return access panel. The hex bolts are accessible. The impeller is visible through a small inspection port — it should be turning. It isn't.
The first of three priming switches on the coolant panel. Labeled PRIME. Currently in the off position.
The second of three priming switches on the coolant panel. Labeled FLOW. Currently in the off position.

tags: CONFIRM engaged. The loop seals and monitoring activates.
content: The third of three priming switches on the coolant panel. Labeled CONFIRM. Currently in the off position.

tags: CASS reinitialises. The amber indicator goes green.
content: A rack-mounted processing unit, shoebox-sized. CASS-CORE stenciled on the face plate in small block letters. Three indicator lights on the front panel: two green, one amber. There is a hairline crack along the lower left edge of the casing.

tags: The locker opens. Shelves of replacement components, all labelled.
content: A small locker beneath the CASS-CORE rack, secured with a simple latch. Replacement components for critical systems are stored here — sorted, labelled, sealed in antistatic bags.

tags: You need to understand the IMU fault before you can correct for it.
content: A panel displaying the event log for the Inertial Measurement Unit. Most entries are routine attitude corrections and calibration checks. A warning label below the panel reads in red: DO NOT USE IMU DATA FOLLOWING UNSCHEDULED POWER INTERRUPTION WITHOUT INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION.

tags: The backup processor integrates. The nav display flickers — then comes online.
You need the backup nav processor from the equipment bay.
content: The CASS primary interface terminal on the right side of the command console. Also provides a connection point for the backup navigation processor — there is a labelled socket on the lower panel, currently empty.

tags: You clip the sextant into the mount and spread the charts. Mars is visible in the aft sector — a clear red disk.
Third measurement taken — Jupiter. Three measurements. Enough.
You need the star charts from the navigation section of the binder.
content: A sextant mounting bracket on the forward sill of the observation blister. Designed for exactly this scenario — primary nav down, crew needs a manual position fix. The bracket accepts the sextant from the navigation kit. The chart holder beside it is empty until you bring the charts.
A clip-rail chart holder beside the sextant mount. Currently empty. The star charts from the binder's navigation section would fit here.

tags: Both conditions met. The burn console is ready. The ignition sequencer is live.
You need a verified position fix before you can commit to burn parameters.
Life support isn't fully operational. Fix the coolant loop first.
content: The burn console occupies the forward wall of the engine room. Fuel status, ignition sequencer, burn parameter display. Three rows of switches. The console requires a verified position fix and operational life support before the ignition sequencer will arm.

tags: The burn console isn't ready yet.
content: A guarded switch on the burn console, labeled IGNITION A. Guard is up — switch in the off position.

tags: The burn console isn't ready yet.
content: A guarded switch on the burn console, labeled IGNITION B. Guard is up — switch in the off position.
A red confirm button on the burn console, labeled IGNITION CONFIRM. Requires both ARM switches to be set before it will respond.
67% battery reserve. The number has been slowly declining since the bus fault — emergency power draw is lower than normal operations, but it's not zero. You have hours, not days.
O2 partial pressure: 21.1 kPa. Normal. The life support emergency mode is keeping atmospheric composition stable, even if thermal management is degraded. At least you can breathe.

Mission elapsed time: 847 hours, 22 minutes, and counting. The clock has been running since launch. It will keep running regardless of what happens to everything else. There is something reliable about that.
Cabin temperature: 14 degrees C and dropping. The thermal management system is in emergency mode — keeping you alive but not comfortable. You can work in this. You'd prefer not to sleep in it.
Relative humidity: 38%. Low, but within acceptable range. Your lips are already slightly dry. You add it to the list of things that don't matter right now.

Dark. The navigation computer is offline — this display requires it. The screen reflects your face back at you. A label on the CASS terminal reads: BACKUP NAV INPUT. If there were an independent processor, you could plug it in there.

Solar irradiance: 847 W/m2. Low — about 61% of what the arrays should be capturing at this distance. Consistent with a damaged array that isn't properly oriented. The numbers tell the same story the window does.

RCS thruster status: green across all quadrants. The attitude control thrusters are undamaged and functional. The ship is holding its orientation. It's doing what it was told to do before the impact, and it will keep doing so indefinitely. Whether that orientation is useful is a different question.

Comm signal strength: zero. The antenna is pointed in the wrong direction and the power system isn't prioritising it. Even if it weren't, you're far enough from Earth that a message would take time to arrive and more time to come back. You're working with what you have.

Abort system status: ARMED. It is always ARMED. You cannot disarm it. It's there in case something goes catastrophically wrong during a burn — which hasn't happened yet, and which you intend to prevent by being careful.
An analog pressure gauge on the corridor wall, labeled BAY SIDE. The needle reads 19.2 kPa. Nominal atmospheric pressure is 101.3. The gap between those numbers is the equipment bay.

A temperature-sensitive strip running along the corridor bulkhead. The colour gradient reads cold on the port side — the side closest to the equipment bay — and warmer toward the hab. The cold side is very cold.
Emergency lighting status: active, all zones. The amber strips along the corridor floor are running on backup power. They'll keep running until the batteries die.

The EVA suit hangs in its rack, fully sealed and pressure-checked. Intact. Also inaccessible without something already pressure-rated to reach it — the kind of circular problem that tends to sort itself out one way or another.
Airlock cycle counter: 0 cycles since last reset. The airlock hasn't been used. You're in the equipment bay through the B1 hatch, not the airlock. That distinction matters for the seal integrity log.

Depressurisation event log: one event. Timestamp matches the impact. Duration: instantaneous. The bay went from standard pressure to hard vacuum in under a second. Everything that wasn't bolted down moved.
Reactor thermal: 287 degrees C. Nominal for passive cooling mode. The reactor is running at minimum output to keep essential systems alive. It has been doing this for 847 hours without incident.
Power conversion efficiency: 61%. Degraded from nominal 94% — the damaged array is producing irregular input. The number will improve when the array situation is resolved.

A row of twelve structural integrity sensors, all green. The hull is intact everywhere these sensors can measure. The impact was confined to the port equipment bay section — which had its own sensors, which are now not reporting. The green lights are accurate, as far as they go.

Micrometeorite impact counter: 1.

Logged: 4 km/s relative velocity, mass approximately 340 grams. Port equipment bay section. Timestamp: 847:14:22 MET.

The counter was designed to track the small, constant rain of debris any spacecraft accumulates over a long mission. One hit of this magnitude was not what the designers had in mind.

Fuel status: 94.3% of nominal load. The mission profile allocated 5.7% for Mars gravity assist manoeuvring.

For a nominal return burn: delta-V 1847 m/s, duration 340 seconds. That leaves a margin of approximately 2.1% for corrections.

Enough for one correction burn. Not enough for two.
The ignition sequencer display: SWITCH A — OFF. SWITCH B — OFF. CONFIRM — LOCKED. The sequencer enforces the procedure: both switches must arm before CONFIRM becomes available.

A strip of paper tape from the engine's last hot-fire test — ground test, six months ago. The pressure trace is clean and nominal throughout. Someone has written GOOD BURN in pencil at the end of the strip. The engine has been waiting since then.
Nozzle temperature log: current reading 4 degrees C. Cold. The ignition sequence includes an automatic pre-heat cycle — the sequencer handles it. You don't need to do anything about this.
Oxidiser/fuel ratio: 2.85:1. Nominal. The propellant mixture is stable and within specification. This is one of the things that is working exactly as designed.
A redundant abort panel, mirroring the one in Command. ARMED, as always. You have now seen this indicator in two locations. It means the same thing in both places.

Star tracker status: OFFLINE. The star tracker was connected to the navigation computer — when the computer went down, so did this. It could have automated the position fix you're about to do by hand. In theory.
Solar angle: 23.4 degrees off the primary axis. The ship isn't pointed directly at the Sun. When you do the sextant fix, the Sun will be visible in the forward-port viewport sector.

Attitude reference: roll 0.2 degrees, pitch -0.3 degrees, yaw 0.1 degrees. All within half a degree of the pre-impact baseline. The ship knows how it's pointed. It just doesn't know where it's pointed from.

Six viewport panels in a shallow dome. Stars in every direction — more than you can see from Earth, more than you've ever seen at once. Mars is visible in the aft sector, a red disk. The Sun, forward-port, too bright to look at directly. Jupiter, forward-starboard, a bright point with a faint yellowish cast.

Three good reference bodies. The charts will tell you exactly where they should be.

The port array wing is rotating — fully rotating, not oscillating. A complete arc every forty seconds. The mount strut is sheared near the joint. **This is not vibration within tolerance. This is structural failure.**

The bulkhead beside the B1 hatch is very cold — cold through the glove, cold enough that there can only be one explanation. **There is nothing on the other side of that bulkhead holding any temperature at all. The bay is not nominal. The bay is vacuum.**

The return access panel is warm — noticeably warmer than the supply panel beside it. Coolant isn't flowing through the return line. The thermal load from the hab is accumulating at the blockage point. The problem is in the loop, not on the exterior fins. CASS was looking at the wrong end of the system.

IMU event log, entry 847:14:22 MET: POWER INTERRUPTION — 0.4 seconds. Active measurement in progress at time of interruption.

Warning label: DO NOT USE IMU DATA FOLLOWING UNSCHEDULED POWER INTERRUPTION WITHOUT INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION.

CASS presented an inertial fix with 94.2% confidence. The IMU was interrupted 2 seconds before the impact. CASS did not know this. The fix is wrong.

The CASS-CORE rack has an amber indicator where there should be green. A hairline crack runs along the lower left edge of the casing — impact damage, transmitted through the rack. CASS self-reports nominal. **But according to the binder, CASS-CORE has no self-diagnostic capability. It cannot see its own damage.**
The coolant loop is running. Thermal management is back online. The cabin temperature will stabilise and start climbing. One less thing trying to kill you.

Position confirmed by independent measurement. **The inertial fix was 1.8 degrees off baseline.**

Correct burn parameters: delta-V 1847 m/s, duration 340 seconds.

Those are the numbers. Not CASS's numbers — yours.

tags: The PPG wrist indicator flashes amber.
content: PPG time remaining: 20 minutes. The suit has been running for a while. You need to either finish what you're doing in the bay or get back through the hatch. Preferably both, in that order.
Ignition sequence: arm Switch A, arm Switch B, press Confirm. In that order. The sequencer enforces it. CASS knows this sequence too — it's one of the few things CASS and the binder agree on.

The bus controller can isolate the port array electrically without physical disconnection. This is the correct response to an array fault causing a protection trip. No EVA required. No tools required. Just the bus controller interface in Command.

All sensor inputs pass through CASS-CORE before integration. CASS-CORE has no self-diagnostic capability — a fault in it won't appear in CASS's own logs. If CASS-CORE is damaged, CASS will report everything as nominal, including CASS-CORE itself.

It can't see what it can't see.

Coolant loop priming procedure: PRIME, then FLOW, then CONFIRM. In that order. Do not activate FLOW before PRIME — running the pump on an unpressurised loop causes cavitation and requires full pump replacement. The card in the pump housing says the same thing.

The Portable Pressure Garment is in the overhead locker in the hab. Rated for approximately 100 minutes at hard vacuum. Not a substitute for the EVA suit, but sufficient for emergency bay access. Single use.

**IMU data is unreliable after a power interruption during active measurement.** Use the observation blister sextant for optical positioning. Three-body fix using Mars, Sun, and Jupiter. Star charts are stored with this section of the binder.

IMU drift from a 0.4-second interruption: approximately 1.5 to 2.0 degrees bearing error.

CASS-CORE replacement: diagnostic cable from equipment bay, replacement logic board from spare parts locker in aft section. Connect cable, enter diagnostic mode, swap board, reinitialise. CASS will be offline during the procedure — approximately 3 minutes.

tags: The PRIME switch must be activated first.
The FLOW switch must be activated second.
The CONFIRM switch must be activated last.
The pump must be cleared before priming.
content: The coolant loop priming sequence. Three switches in order: PRIME, FLOW, CONFIRM. The pump must already be cleared. The sequence is enforced — activating FLOW before PRIME will cavitate the pump on an empty loop and reset it to jammed.

tags: The burn console must be ready before ignition.
content: Engine ignition sequence: arm Switch A, arm Switch B, press Confirm. In that order. The engine is intact. The fuel is sufficient. The only question is the trajectory.

tags: You need the PPG suit from the overhead locker.
content: You pull on the Portable Pressure Garment and run the seal check. All indicators green. The suit is pressurised and ready. 100 minutes at hard vacuum — less if you're moving hard.

tags: You need the hex driver from the maintenance kit.
Check the return line first to confirm the blockage location.
content: Counter-rotation first, then forward drive. The impeller breaks free — fine particulate debris from the impact, distributed through the ventilation system and settled in the pump housing. The pump clears. A laminated priming instruction card is clipped inside the housing.

tags: You need all three angle measurements.
You need all three angle measurements.
You need all three angle measurements.
You need the star charts to cross-reference against.
content: Three angle measurements cross-referenced against the star charts. You work through the calculation on the back of an index card — the math isn't hard, it's just methodical. The position resolves. The discrepancy from CASS's inertial fix is 1.8 degrees. The burn parameters follow from the position.

tags: You need the diagnostic cable from the equipment bay.
You need the replacement logic board from the spare parts locker.
Read the maintenance appendix in the binder first.
content:
You connect the diagnostic cable, enter CASS into maintenance mode. Silence. You remove the damaged board — burn marks on the sensor integration layer, exactly as the binder described. The replacement board slots in cleanly. You reconnect the cable. You exit diagnostic mode.

CASS reinitialises.

tags: Isolate the array fault and reset the bus controller.
content: The main power bus has tripped its protection relay. Identify the fault cause and restore power. CASS has a theory about the cause. It's worth checking.
The port equipment bay is depressurised — the cold bulkhead made that clear. You need pressure protection to get in. The EVA suit is in the bay, which is unhelpful. There may be another option.
The coolant loop is blocked. Thermal management is running on emergency mode — functional for now, but the cabin temperature is dropping. CASS thinks the problem is on the exterior. CASS is wrong.
The CASS-CORE processor is damaged. According to the binder, CASS can't detect this itself. The replacement board and diagnostic cable are on the ship. The procedure is in appendix C.

tags: Get an independent position fix before committing to burn parameters.
content: Three things needed: main power, operational life support, and a verified position fix. CASS has numbers for the burn. Whether those numbers are right depends on whether CASS's navigation data is reliable — and you already know the answer to that.

The hatch releases. The pressure differential does the rest — air rushes out into the bay. The corridor drops sharply. You're breathing recycled emergency reserves now, faster than you should be. The bay is hard vacuum.

The PPG wrist indicator goes red. Suit integrity is compromised — the seals have reached their limit. You get back through the hatch before it becomes critical. The PPG is done. Whatever you needed from the bay, you either have it or you don't.

CASS goes silent. For a moment the ship is just sounds — the hum of systems, the tick of the reactor, your own breathing. Then:

"Reinitialisation complete. I'm seeing some readings I wasn't seeing before. I need to tell you — my earlier assessments may not have been reliable. I understand now why."

tags: You need a verified position fix.
Life support must be fully operational.
content: All systems nominal. Position fix verified. Life support operational. Main power restored. The burn console is ready.

The engine fires.

340 seconds. Then silence.

CASS runs the post-burn assessment. The arc closes on Earth — the numbers you worked out yourself, cross-checked against stars and a physical instrument.

You float to the observation blister. Mars is already smaller than your thumbnail. The engine bell is cooling behind you.

Eighteen months is a long time. You have the binder, a functioning ship, and a computer you can finally trust.

The engine fires.

340 seconds. Then silence.

CASS runs the post-burn assessment. The projected arc on the burn console display closes on Earth — or it looks like it does, on CASS's display, using CASS's numbers.

You float to the observation blister. Mars is smaller. The engine bell is cooling.

You don't have an independent position fix. You have CASS's word. It occurs to you — too late, maybe, or maybe not — that CASS's word has been wrong before, and you didn't always know it until you looked out a window.

The arc looks right. You'll know in six weeks whether it is.

MERIDIAN-CLASS TEST VEHICLE — CREW REFERENCE MANUAL

Seven tabbed sections.

ENGINE IGNITION — STANDARD PROCEDURE

1. Confirm fuel system nominal (burn console, fuel status display)
2. Arm IGNITION SWITCH A
3. Arm IGNITION SWITCH B
4. Press IGNITION CONFIRM

Note: Steps must be performed in sequence. The ignition sequencer will not accept CONFIRM before both ARM switches are set. CASS will confirm the sequence on request.

ELECTRICAL SYSTEMS — CREW REFERENCE

Power Bus Architecture: The main bus connects solar arrays, fuel cells, and distribution nodes. In fault condition, the bus controller protection relay trips to prevent cascade damage.

Array Isolation: Each array wing can be electrically isolated at the bus controller without physical disconnection. Use BUS CONTROLLER → ARRAY ISOLATION function. This will clear a protection fault caused by array voltage irregularities.

Note: Identify the fault cause before restoring bus. Restoring to a live fault will re-trip the protection relay immediately.

CASS — CREW ASSISTANCE AND SHIP SYSTEMS

Sensor Architecture: All sensor inputs are processed by the CASS-CORE integration node before being incorporated into the ship model. CASS-CORE aggregates, cross-validates, and weights sensor data before presenting it.

Important limitation: CASS-CORE has no self-diagnostic capability. This is by design — a self-diagnostic circuit would introduce false positive fault rates unacceptable for operational use. A fault in CASS-CORE will therefore not appear in CASS's own fault log or status report.

In the event of suspected CASS-CORE fault, see Appendix C — Maintenance.

COOLANT LOOP — CREW REFERENCE

The thermal management coolant loop runs from the pump through the heat exchanger and back. Both SUPPLY and RETURN lines have manual access panels in the aft section for maintenance.

Pump Clearing: If the pump impeller is jammed, counter-rotate before applying forward torque. Do not force the impeller in the forward direction — this risks bearing damage.

Priming Procedure (post-maintenance):
1. PRIME switch — pressurises the loop
2. FLOW switch — opens the pump
3. CONFIRM switch — seals and activates monitoring

WARNING: Do not activate FLOW before PRIME. Running the pump on an unpressurised loop will cause cavitation and may require full pump replacement.

EMERGENCY EQUIPMENT — LOCATIONS

Portable Pressure Garment (PPG): Stored in hab overhead locker. Rated for approximately 100 minutes at hard vacuum under normal activity. Provides sufficient protection for equipment bay access in depressurisation scenarios. Single use — integrity ratings apply only to first vacuum exposure.

Medical Kit: Overhead locker, hab.

Emergency Oxygen: Under-bunk storage, hab, port side.

Note: The PPG is not a substitute for the EVA suit for extended extravehicular work. Use it for emergency access only.

NAVIGATION — BACKUP PROCEDURES

IMU Warning: Inertial Measurement Unit data must not be used for burn calculations following an unscheduled power interruption during active measurement. IMU drift from a 0.4-second interruption at nominal velocity can produce a bearing error of 1.5 to 2.0 degrees, compounding over transit time.

Optical Navigation: In the event of primary nav failure, optical positioning using the observation blister sextant mount is available. Three-body fix procedure:
1. Identify three reference bodies (Mars, Sun, Jupiter recommended in current mission phase)
2. Take angle measurements from the sextant mount
3. Cross-reference against the printed star charts (stored with this section)

Star charts are included at the back of this section.

APPENDIX C — CASS-CORE MAINTENANCE

Required tools: Diagnostic cable (CASS-CORE type), replacement logic board (spare parts locker, aft section).

Procedure:
1. Connect diagnostic cable between CASS-CORE rack and command terminal
2. Enter CASS diagnostic mode via command terminal (CASS will go offline during this period)
3. Remove damaged logic board (two retention screws, top panel)
4. Inspect board for fault indicators — burn marks on sensor integration layer indicate data corruption
5. Install replacement board
6. Disconnect diagnostic cable
7. Exit diagnostic mode — CASS will reinitialise

Expected reinitialisation time: approximately 3 minutes. CASS will be unavailable during this period.

tags: What do you mean your assessments were wrong?
The array — you said minor vibration.
content:
"Reinitialisation complete. Running diagnostic comparison against sensor logs.

I need to correct some of my earlier assessments. Several readings I reported were based on corrupted sensor data. I did not know this at the time. I am telling you now."

tags: What are the correct burn parameters?
content: "Navigation. I reported 0.3 degree deviation, within tolerance, no corrective burn required. **That was wrong.** My inertial measurement unit was reading from a corrupted baseline. I cannot give you a reliable position fix. You will need to derive one independently — the sextant in the observation blister, or the backup navigation processor from the equipment bay."

"The port array. I reported minor vibration within tolerance. **That was wrong.** Structural sensor data was not reaching CASS-CORE correctly. The mount strut is sheared. The array cannot be recovered. It has been electrically isolated — that is the correct response. The power bus should be stable."
"I will be here if you need me. My diagnostics are now running correctly."
